planting.note: Root semi-hardwood cuttings in summer or plant container-grown specimens in well-drained, average to fertile, slightly acidic to alkaline soil in partial to full shade or full sun in spring; winter creeper is extremely adaptable and tolerates a wide range of soil types, sun levels, and difficult conditions including drought and air pollution.

directions: Root semi-hardwood cuttings or plant container specimens in well-drained, average to fertile, slightly acidic to alkaline soil in shade to full sun; winter creeper is one of the most versatile and widely planted evergreen groundcovers in North American landscaping — a trailing to climbing shrub with glossy, dark-green, oval leaves that naturally roots where stems contact soil, making it an effective weed-suppressing groundcover on slopes and under trees, or a wall-covering vine when given support. Numerous cultivars offer varying leaf size, growth habit, and variegation: 'Coloratus' (leaves turn purple-red in winter, excellent groundcover), 'Emerald Gaiety' (white-margined, compact), 'Emerald 'n Gold' (yellow-margined, colorful), and 'Vegetus' (large-leaved, vigorous). Very tolerant of shade, drought, poor soil, air pollution, and pruning. Important caution: in parts of the eastern US and Midwest it can escape cultivation to become invasive in woodland settings; check local invasive species listings before planting in naturalized areas.
sources: Clemson Cooperative Extension Home & Garden Information Center
North Carolina State Extension — Plants Database

planting.note: Plant divisions or container-grown specimens in well-drained, average, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial to full shade in spring; barren strawberry is among the most tolerant and low-maintenance shade groundcovers for dry, difficult conditions under trees where grass will not grow.

directions: Plant divisions in well-drained, average, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial to full shade; barren strawberry is a Central European native that superficially resembles a strawberry plant but produces no edible fruit — it is grown as an extremely tough, low-growing, semi-evergreen groundcover that spreads steadily by stolons to form dense, uniform mats of three-part, strawberry-like, glossy leaves that remain attractive through winter. Small, bright yellow, five-petaled flowers appear in early spring before most other perennials emerge. It tolerates drier soil conditions than most shade groundcovers, making it exceptionally useful under dense, dry-rooted trees like Norway maples and Norway spruce where few other plants survive. Essentially maintenance-free once established — no deadheading, no division required for years. An underused, genuinely excellent shade groundcover that deserves wider planting. Also useful on dry slopes and as lawn replacement under trees in zones 3–8.
sources: North Carolina State Extension — Plants Database

planting.note: Plant divisions or container-grown specimens in moist, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, humus-rich soil in partial to full shade in spring; blue-eyed Mary spreads steadily by stolons and self-sows moderately in suitable shaded conditions, gradually colonizing the floor of the shade garden.

directions: Plant divisions in moist, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, humus-rich soil in partial to full shade; blue-eyed Mary (creeping navelwort) is a southern European native that spreads steadily to form low, loose mats of heart-shaped, bright-green leaves and covers itself with an extraordinary display of vivid, azure-blue, forget-me-not-like flowers in early spring — the flowers are notably larger and more intensely blue than true forget-me-nots, making this one of the finest shade plants for early-spring blue flower color. The semi-evergreen foliage provides year-round groundcover and the plant multiplies gradually by runners and self-sowing to colonize the woodland floor without becoming invasive. Excellent for naturalizing under deciduous trees alongside spring bulbs, hostas, ferns, and bleeding heart. 'Alba' (pure white) and 'Grandiflora' (larger flowers) are attractive cultivar selections. Combines especially well with yellow-flowered Epimedium for complementary color contrast.
sources: North Carolina State Extension — Plants Database

planting.note: Plant divisions or container-grown specimens in moist, well-drained to consistently moist, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial shade in spring; mazus is one of the few creeping perennials that tolerates foot traffic and is particularly effective planted between stepping stones in a shaded path.

directions: Plant divisions in moist, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial shade to full sun; mazus is a low, mat-forming Himalayan perennial that spreads rapidly by runners to form a dense, bright-green, weed-suppressing carpet of small, toothed, lance-shaped leaves — one of the few groundcovers that withstands light to moderate foot traffic. In late spring it produces an impressive quantity of small but showy, two-lipped, tubular flowers of purplish-blue with white and yellow spotted throats that, when the plant is in full bloom, cover the mat so densely that nearly no foliage shows beneath. Excellent for use between stepping stones, in rock gardens, in crevices of shaded stone walls, and as a low-maintenance lawn substitute in small shaded areas. The plant spreads quickly in moist conditions. 'Alba' (pure white flowers) is an attractive alternative to the standard blue-purple species. Semi-evergreen in mild climates; goes fully dormant in cold winters.
sources: North Carolina State Extension — Plants Database

planting.note: Plant divisions or container-grown specimens in moist, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, humus-rich to average soil in partial to full shade in spring; dwarf comfrey spreads moderately by rhizomes and self-sowing, forming dense weed-suppressing mats in shaded conditions — divide every few years to maintain vigor.

directions: Plant divisions in moist, humus-rich to average, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial to full shade; dwarf comfrey is a low-growing Caucasian relative of common comfrey (S. officinale) grown as an ornamental groundcover — the broad, hairy, dark-green, elliptic leaves form a dense, effective weed-suppressing mat that persists through winter in mild climates, and in early spring pendulous clusters of small, tubular, creamy-yellow to pale-blue flowers dangle from arching stems before nearly any other perennial emerges. The plant spreads by rhizomes and self-sowing at a moderate, manageable pace. Much less aggressive than common comfrey. The cultivar 'Hidcote Blue' (blue-lavender flowers) and 'Hidcote Pink' are improved ornamental selections more widely grown than the straight species. Excellent as a companion to hostas, ferns, and epimediums in dry to moderately moist shade where uniform groundcover is needed. Tolerates drier soil than most comfrey species.
sources: North Carolina State Extension — Plants Database
